claim: A tool shall emit a deterministic, LLM-free shortlist of SETTLED requirement pairs that might hide an unmediated tension.
why: The framework held tensions well but historically did not FIND them (0 of 8 conflicts machine-surfaced over its whole history; latent-mining covered 0 pairs; 3 axes never fired). This tool closes the missing first-act-of-sight half of the mediation loop by sweeping the WHOLE settled graph. The capability is realized as `hotam inspect` (internal/diagnose/inspect.go): a deterministic, LLM-free advisory that surfaces tension candidates via four heuristics - InspectSharedAssumptionClusters (pairs sharing a DEAD/HOLDS assumption that one alone cannot break), InspectEntityStateConflicts (two requirements asserting incompatible states on the same entity), InspectLexicalClaimOverlap (natural-language claim pairs with high token overlap but different owners), and InspectAxisCoReference (requirements co-referencing the same Axis with opposing pull). Each surfaced Candidate carries its heuristic name, the member ids, an evidence string, and a score, so a human can review the shortlist. (Historical note: the original tool was tools/audit_tensions.py, which used different heuristics - POLE axis-pole pull / MODAL prohibition-vs-obligation / NOUN cross-owner overlap; the current inspect heuristics reimagines the same first-act-of-sight with graph-structural signals better suited to the current ontology, and those pole/modal/noun tests were not carried forward.) Deterministic and stdlib-only so the shortlist is reproducible and gate-safe (exit code always 0; never gates). PROSE (not ENFORCED): the shortlist is an advisory tool with no enforcer test asserting its heuristics fire on specific fixtures (the inspect heuristics are exercised by table-driven tests in inspect_test.go but as correctness checks, not as enforcement of this requirement). ENFORCED 2026-07-13: TestInspect_DeterministicShortlist in internal/diagnose/inspect_test.go mechanically verifies this claim. NOISE FIX 2026-07-13 (external review, P1): on the real hotam-spec-self graph InspectLexicalClaimOverlap was over-firing (1231 of 1232 total candidates), because its stop-word list is generic English closed-class vocabulary and cannot catch a self-describing methodology's own frequently-repeated open-class words (e.g. "requirement" at 12.4% document frequency, "graph" 19.7%, "every" 26.5% across the 234 SETTLED claims) -- two claims sharing only such words were being scored as topically related when they were not. Fixed by computing a document-frequency table over the loaded graph's own SETTLED claims at call time (corpusCommonTokens in internal/diagnose/inspect.go) and excluding any token appearing in more than CorpusCommonTokenFraction (5%) of SETTLED claims from the shared-token overlap count, exactly like a stop word -- this is derived fresh from whatever domain graph is loaded, never a hardcoded word list, so it stays content-free across domains (R-content-free-no-business-data) and adapts automatically to each domain's own vocabulary. Below MinCorpusSizeForFrequencyFilter (8) SETTLED requirements the exclusion is a no-op and behavior is unchanged, since frequency is not a meaningful signal on tiny corpora. Confirmed via total-candidate count on the real graph: 1231 lexical_claim_overlap candidates before -> 234 after (81% reduction), spot-checked to confirm the dropped pairs shared only corpus-common connective words (e.g. "never", "framework", "resolver" alone) with no real topical anchor, while the surviving top-scoring candidates now carry genuinely narrow shared vocabulary. `hotam confront` reuses the same exclusion (Confront/confrontHit in internal/diagnose/confront.go), so a candidate sharing only corpus-common words with a SETTLED/REJECTED requirement no longer surfaces as a duplicate/re-litigation suspect. This does NOT guarantee zero false negatives: a real tension whose only shared vocabulary happens to be corpus-common, with no opposite marker and no rarer shared token, is still missed -- the same class of miss the plain stop-word list already accepted for generic English words, now extended to a domain's own frequent words. FALSE-NEGATIVE VALIDATION 2026-07-13 (task #99, independent follow-up review): checked all 8 real resolver-confirmed Conflict node member pairs in this graph's own `conflicts` array against InspectLexicalClaimOverlap's output, both with the corpus-frequency filter active and with it disabled (stop-words-only, the pre-fix behavior) via a scratch harness reusing corpusCommonTokens/claimTokens/markerHits directly. Result: 7 of the 8 pairs never fired via lexical overlap even with the filter fully disabled -- they clear neither MinLexicalOverlapTokens nor the owner/marker gate on raw shared-token count alone, consistent with this requirement's own "0 of 8 conflicts machine-surfaced" history note; they were found by the resolver through other means entirely, not a filter regression. Exactly ONE pair is a proven corpus-frequency false negative: C-c3911f28 (R-content-free-framework + R-empty-content-is-legitimate) shares 3 tokens ("content" 5.5%, "graph" 19.9%, "spec" 7.6% document frequency) across different owners and clears the firing bar pre-filter, but all three tokens sit over the 5% ceiling and are excluded, dropping shared tokens to 0 post-filter. A threshold sweep showed rescuing it is not worth the cost: raising CorpusCommonTokenFraction to 0.06 alone (letting "content" back in) grows total lexical_claim_overlap candidates from 232 to 316 (+36%); fully rescuing all three tokens needs 0.20, which explodes candidates to 1186, undoing nearly all of the 1231->234 noise fix this note describes above. Accepted as a documented tradeoff rather than tuned: a Conflict node is already graph ground truth (no heuristic needs to "discover" it), and inspect's stated purpose is surfacing NEW undiscovered tension candidates, not reproducing every already-known Conflict. Separately verified the `hotam confront "The system shall enforce every requirement structurally"` hit against R-requirement-freshness-fields flagged by the same review: the shared tokens "system" (2.1% document frequency) and "structurally" (0.8%) are both genuinely rare, well under the 5% ceiling -- this is NOT corpus-filter noise leaking through, it is a real (if weak, score=2, the minimum bar) overlap correctly surfaced by the tool's own stated over-inclusive-by-design philosophy (MinLexicalOverlapTokens's doc comment: cheap false positives preferred over hidden true tensions), left as-is. Both findings pinned by TestInspectLexicalClaimOverlap_KnownConflictGroundTruth (internal/diagnose/inspect_test.go), which loads the real domain graph, checks every real Conflict member pair against InspectLexicalClaimOverlap's live output, and fails loud if a NEW Conflict pair silently joins the miss set beyond the manually-verified allow-list (knownLexicalOverlapMissByDesign) -- the same false-negative risk this requirement's NOISE FIX note already flagged as unverified is now backed by an exact, reproducible count instead of a hypothetical caveat.
